title: Your first function
successMessage: One function, three calls. Change the format in greet() and all three outputs update. Functions defined before main() are visible throughout. Functions after main() need a forward declaration.

title: Pass by value — copies are made
instruction:
In C++, function parameters are **copied** by default. Changing them inside the function doesn't affect the caller:

```cpp
void addFive(int x) {   // x is a COPY
    x += 5;             // doesn't change caller's variable
}
```

This is safe but slow for large objects.
successMessage: x is unchanged! The function received a copy of 10, doubled the copy to 20, and discarded it when it returned. Pass-by-value is the default in C++ — safe, no side effects.

title: Pass by reference — modify the original
instruction:
Add `&` to make a parameter a **reference** — the function operates on the original variable:

```cpp
void doubleIt(int& n) {  // & = reference, not a copy
    n = n * 2;           // modifies the caller's variable!
}
```

Also use `const string&` to pass large objects efficiently without allowing modification.

codeChecks.message: Use int& reference parameter: void doubleIt(int& n)
Use int& for both swap parameters.

successMessage: x went from 10 to 20 — the reference let the function modify the original. swap uses two references to exchange values. const string& avoids copying without the function being able to modify it.

title: Default arguments
successMessage: Default arguments make functions flexible without overloading. Defaults at the end — that rule is enforced by the compiler. Log functions with default INFO level are a classic use case.

title: Function overloading
instruction:
Multiple functions with the same name but different parameter types or counts.

The compiler picks the right one based on the arguments.
successMessage: Four functions named add — the compiler picks by argument types. Overloading is how C++ standard library functions like abs(), min(), max() work on any numeric type.

title: Recursion
instruction:
A function that calls itself. Every recursive function needs:
1. **Base case** — stops the recursion
2. **Recursive case** — calls itself with a smaller problem

`factorial(5) = 5 × factorial(4) = ... = 120`
successMessage: 10! = 3,628,800. Fibonacci: 0,1,1,2,3,5... Each number is the sum of the previous two. Recursion is elegant — but the fibonacci implementation here is O(2^n). In practice, use memoization or iteration.

title: Build string utilities
instruction:
No starter code. Write three functions:

- `bool isPalindrome(const string& s)` — true if s reads the same backwards (case-insensitive hint: compare lowercase copies)
- `int countOccurrences(const string& s, char target)` — count how many times char appears
- `string reverse(const string& s)` — return reversed string

Test each with two inputs.
hint: For isPalindrome: use string reversed(s.rbegin(), s.rend()); and compare. For countOccurrences: loop and count.

codeChecks.message: Define int countOccurrences(const string& s, char target).

successMessage: Three string utilities using const string& — efficient, no unnecessary copies. string(s.rbegin(), s.rend()) reverses using reverse iterators. Chapter 7 done!
